Thanks for the feedback! We (my team) uses the library for reporting project deployment activity and continuous integration notifications to our Slack account. So I started using your library to interface with our python scripts.

The organisation I work for has a proxy for outgoing traffic so I integrated pyslack library with our scripts and configured scripts to use the proxy settings. So I thought I should modify the library to work with/without a proxy as it solves our problems of getting through the proxy.

We also have a more complicated proxy configuration for production servers where we use pyslack to report mission critical messages to the developer channel. And the changes in this PR fixes the problem of getting though complicated proxy configurations.

Cheers.

Btw it works if I use the `Popen(exp_arg, stdout=PIPE)` inside a python class that has the `objfunction` function. And then pass its object to a `BasicWorkerThread`. 

Example, `TorchOptim` is a class that has the needed properties (similar to the `Ackley` class in the examples), then I can call this:

```
data = TorchOptim()

# Create a strategy and a controller
controller = ThreadController()
controller.strategy = \
    SyncStrategyNoConstraints(
        worker_id=0, data=data,
        maxeval=maxeval, nsamples=nsamples,
        exp_design=LatinHypercube(dim=data.dim, npts=2*(data.dim+1)),
        response_surface=RBFInterpolant(surftype=CubicRBFSurface, maxp=maxeval),
        sampling_method=CandidateDYCORS(data=data, numcand=100*data.dim))

# Launch the threads and give them access to the objective function
for _ in range(nthreads):
    worker = BasicWorkerThread(controller, data.objfunction)
    controller.launch_worker(worker)

# Run the optimization strategy
result = controller.run()

```

Inside the `objfunction` function I'm calling the external process via `Popen` and then the program works as intended.

> Does +if (CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR STREQUAL CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR) this automatically set the EXPORT variable under a certain condition

Yes. If the `glad` source dir is the "root" of the project, it will *not* export the variables to the parent project (because there is none).

If the `glad` source dir is *not* the "root" of the project, for example, it was added by a call to `add_subdirectory(glad)` and glad is a git-submodule (in my case), then it will export the variables to the parent project.

The `CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR` is the directory of the current CMakeLists.txt file. The `CMAKE_SOURCE_DIR` is the directory of the root CMakeLists.txt file.

First of all `find_packages()` works with Python packages only, so regular files like `test-requirements.txt` are not processed by this function regardless of the `exclude` argument. Contents of `requirements.txt` are included in `requires.txt` in egg-info because of `install_requires` directive.

Next, when checking what packages to exclude `find_packages()` matches their names against patterns specified in the `exclude` argument using `fnmatchcase()`. Thus 'test' pattern only excludes the topmost `test` package in your repo (it is a package because it has `__init__.py`), but not, for example, `test.ast` package. See for yourself:
```
>>> from fnmatch import fnmatchcase
>>> fnmatchcase('test', 'test')
True
>>> fnmatchcase('test.ast', 'test')
False
>>> fnmatchcase('test', 'test*')
True
>>> fnmatchcase('test.ast', 'test*')
True
```

The daemon argument to new_task() is used to create a daemonic task.  It must be specified by keyword only and can only be used inside a coroutine (which is not evident in the code example on this issue).

It seems that using queues are also a technique for communicating between Tkinter and threads.   

In addressing this, I've been doing a lot of thinking about the Curio Queue and Event objects.  Right now there are synchronous `put()` and `set()` methods.   However, maybe they are completely unneeded--actions that need to be awaited can be queued up in a deque and processed later.   This is much more general purpose and flexible.     So, I would encourage you to use that technique instead of the Queue object.  I'm probably going to remove the synchronous implementation of those methods (which were always highly experimental and not really documented).

This seems to break for some unit types

```
In [39]: x = Decimal(100) * ureg.nm

In [40]: x.to('m')
Out[40]: <Quantity(1.00E-7, 'meter')>

In [41]: x = Decimal(100) * ureg.nF

In [42]: x.to('F')
Out[42]: <Quantity(1.000000000000000300E-7, 'farad')>
```

There is an unavoidable name clash between that and nanometer. The only way would be to create a restricted definition set for your purpose, removing nanometer. But might hamper the usage for other purposes.

But the function's unit is e.g. 10uA. A number 1 would mean 10uA. A number 2 would mean 20uA. If I use pint and forward 20uA to the wrapper, the actual value given to the function should be 2. For a return value of 2, the wrapper should give me 20uA. 

I've made a pull request which allows to do that.

Sometimes functions (in C) have such strange units, because it allows to adjust the range of wanted physical current to fit into for example a uint16_t.

You need to define the other equation. See for example:

```
@context boltzmann
    [temperature] -> [energy]: boltzmann_constant * value
    [energy] -> [temperature]: value / boltzmann_constant
@end
```

To clarify: minimap2 gives chimeric alignment for all types of sequences.
You can do `bedtools bamtobed`, followed by `bedtools bedtobam` to remove the CIGAR and sequence information from reads.

The latest minimap2 has an --split-prefix option:
```sh
minimap2 -a --split-prefix tmp ref.fa query.fa
```
With this option, minimap2 won't write a unmapped record multiple times.

Figure out the difference between:
- measurement rate: Up to 144hz.
- report rate: In stream mode, reduces the number of samples reported to the computer. Default is 1 (don't need to change). If set, number of samples reported is `samples/report rate`
- RS232 data rate: if greater than the measurement rate, we get duplicate records. If less, records are dropped

After further digging, this issue is unrelated to IceCream.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

class Foo:
    def __repr__(self):
        return u'Something ç'
print(Foo())
```

Yields

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./foo.py", line 8, in <module>
    print(Foo())
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\xe7' in position 10: ordinal not in range(128)
```

The problem occurs when `__repr__()` returns a unicode object in Python 2. See

> __repr__() is not really allowed to return Unicode objects in Python 2.x. If
> you do this, you're on your own. The CPython internals try to convert any
> non-str object to a str object, but this is only done to assure that
> PyObject_Repr() always returns a str object.
> 
> I'd suggest closing this as won't fix.

of

  * https://bugs.python.org/issue5876

Is it your code's `__repr__()` that returns a Unicode object? Or SQLAlchemy's?
At first glance, `SQLAlchemy.Model.__repr__()` appears to return a str, not a
Unicode object.

I'm not going to send a PR because my simulations show that stop losses are losing me money.  I will dig a little, as I believe I may have a bug, but it's likely that I'll give up on using stop loss orders. 

Below is what I implemented, in case anyone finds it useful. It supports regular & trailing stop loss orders, takes a threshold as parameter, and supports a "fallback" ticker to use instead of cash. The trailing stoploss mode needs a custom Rebalance() function that sets    ``` target.perm['stoploss_set_up_on'][item[0]] = target.now```.

Closing request: bt's design is quite sufficient for stop loss orders after all.

```python
class StopLoss(bt.Algo):
    """
    Implements a stop loss (regular or trailing). Sells assets if they go below a certain threshold under their maximum value since they were bought.

    Args:
        * threshold (float): threshold of loss that triggers sale. Use 0.05 for 5%.
        * trailing (bool): implement a trailing stop loss if true (use the highest price as reference). 
                           If false, implement a regular stop loss (use the buy price as reference).
        * target_ticker (string): name of the ticker to which to reallocate capital when stoploss orders are triggered.
                           By default, keep proceeds in cash.
    """

    def __init__(self, threshold, trailing=False, target_ticker=None):
        super(StopLoss, self).__init__()
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.trailing = trailing
        self.target_ticker = target_ticker

    def __call__(self, target):
        # For each current position, check its drawdown starting on the date it was purchased

        # If no stoploss was previously set, bail
        if target.perm.get('stoploss_set_up_on') == None:
            target.perm['stoploss_set_up_on'] = {}
            return True

        # Build list of currently held securities
        l = [ target.children[cname] for cname in target.children if target.children[cname]._weight > 0 ]
        for c in l:
            try:
                stoploss_from = target.perm['stoploss_set_up_on'][c.name]
            except KeyError:
                continue
            if not self.trailing:
                # Regular stop loss
                price_base = c.prices.ix[stoploss_from]
                loss = (c.price / price_base) - 1
                triggered = loss < -self.threshold
            else:
                # Trailing stop loss, use ffn's to_drawdown_series
                d = c.prices.ix[stoploss_from:target.now].to_drawdown_series()
                d = d[d < -self.threshold]
                triggered = (len(d) > 0)
                
            if triggered:
                print("Triggering stoploss ",  c.name, target.now, c.price, stoploss_from, c.value)
                reallocate_weight = c.weight
                target.close(c.name)
                if (self.target_ticker):
                    target.rebalance(reallocate_weight, self.target_ticker)

        return True
```

The conversations created in the edge case described above - without events - may not be loaded by the official Clients on init after _some hours_, this also applies to the hangups ui. They are loaded in the background as they receive focus/watermarks/typing activity until they receive a _normal_ `ConversationEvent` and get displayed as usual.

```
def _calc_traceback_limit(tb):
	"""Calculates limit-parameter to strip away pytypes' internals when used
	with API from traceback module.
	"""
	limit = 1
	tb2 = tb
	while not tb2.tb_next is None:
		try:
			maybe_pytypes = tb2.tb_next.tb_frame.f_code.co_filename.split(os.sep)[-2]
		except IndexError:
			maybe_pytypes = None
		if maybe_pytypes == 'pytypes' and not \
				tb2.tb_next.tb_frame.f_code == pytypes.typechecker._pytypes___import__.__code__:
			break
		else:
			limit += 1
			tb2 = tb2.tb_next
	return limit
```

@Poogles My opinion hasn't changed, and [everything I've said before](https://github.com/jsocol/pystatsd/issues/83#issuecomment-266334518) remains true. In particular, at best, it's a parameter that does nothing, and at worst, it prevents metrics from being recorded.

Having spent a lot of time working with DataDog over the past couple years, I'm even more convinced. If you have tags, it fundamentally changes the way you name metrics and think about aggregation. With tags, you aggregate by default and disaggregate via tags; but without them, you have to reverse that. That is not something that should be adopted lightly, and I think is even more reason to make the opt in to that behavior _extremely_ explicit. Requiring a new client to add/remove tags makes you touch every file that uses it, which is still below the minimum level of thought to put into the switch.

One thing in your example that was really confusing is that you use main_thread as a function name, but in fact, you're running that function in the child threads.  Typically, people refer to "main thread" and "main()" function together, meaning the parent thread + function that launches all the child threads.

Also, you can create connection handler objects and pass them into functions on child threads, (e.g. so you can re-use a ssh session) but you better be really sure that you're being threadsafe (only reading/writing the object from one thread at a time).   This is really easy to screw up.

Also, don't use global vars.  That's a code smell unless you really know what you're doing with them.

This is expected behavior; it is a mathematical result, rather than just something about the code.  There are two things you are doing "wrong" with your test function which makes it impossible for a finite expansion in `s=1` spin-weighted spherical harmonics (SWSHs) to reproduce your input.

First, you are demanding that the function have spin weight 1, but a nonzero average value.  Fields of nonzero spin weight must have average value 0, since modes with `ell < abs(s)` are necessarily zero by the nature of spin-weighted fields.

Second, you can't expect to resolve a discontinuous function very well with just `lmax=8`. 
What you're seeing is related to [the Gibbs phenomenon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gibbs_phenomenon).  Just as a truncated Fourier expansion of a step function has wiggles, the truncated SWSH expansion of your two-dimensional step function has two-dimensional wiggles.  To see this more clearly, you can increase the resolution, and you'll find that the errors are mostly at the edges of your nonzero pixel.

There's a little subtlety here: as you add pixels, if you keep defining your function with `f_in[2, 4] = 1.0 + 1.0j`, you're changing the function.  So I'll redefine your function as being `1.0 + 1.0j` anywhere in the space occupied by the `[2, 4]` pixel when `lmax=8`:
```python
theta_phi_8 = np.array([[[theta, phi]
                         for phi in np.linspace(0.0, 2*np.pi, num=2*8+1, endpoint=False)]
                        for theta in np.linspace(0.0, np.pi, num=2*8+1, endpoint=True)])
theta_min = (theta_phi_8[1, 4, 0] + theta_phi_8[2, 4, 0])/2
theta_max = (theta_phi_8[2, 4, 0] + theta_phi_8[3, 4, 0])/2
phi_min = (theta_phi_8[2, 3, 1] + theta_phi_8[2, 4, 1])/2
phi_max = (theta_phi_8[2, 4, 1] + theta_phi_8[2, 5, 1])/2

def f(theta, phi):
    if (theta > theta_min and theta < theta_max and
        phi > phi_min and phi < phi_max):
        return 1.0 + 1.0j
    else:
        return 0.0j
```
Then, we can remake your images above with a higher `lmax`:
```python

lmax = 256
f_in = np.array([[f(theta, phi)
                  for phi in np.linspace(0.0, 2*np.pi, num=2*lmax+1, endpoint=False)]
                 for theta in np.linspace(0.0, np.pi, num=2*lmax+1, endpoint=True)])
alm2 = spinsfast.map2salm(f_in, s, lmax)
f_out = spinsfast.salm2map(alm2, s, lmax, 2*lmax+1, 2*lmax+1)
print("Maximum deviation: ", np.max(abs(f_in - f_out))/abs(np.max(f_in)))
plt.close('all')
fig, axes = plt.subplots(figsize=[18, 4], ncols=3)
draw(axes[0], f_in.real, 'input')
draw(axes[1], f_out.real, 'output')
draw(axes[2], abs(f_in - f_out), 'difference')
plt.show()
```
`Maximum deviation:  0.514373151752`
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1470769/28534006-1da6bca4-706d-11e7-80be-3765be6b683d.png)
As expected, the errors are localized around that original pixel.

A much better test is to see if the difference converges to smaller values as you increase the `lmax` value.  Just as with Gibbs phenomena, we don't expect the *maximum* value of the difference to keep getting smaller with increasing resolution; we only expect the L2 norm of the difference to get smaller.  As with most spectral methods, I would expect roughly exponential convergence.  So, with those caveats, the following code is a better test for your function:
```python
pixel_area = - (phi_max - phi_min) * (np.cos(theta_max) - np.cos(theta_min))
f_average = (1.0 + 1.0j) * pixel_area / (4*np.pi)

ells = np.arange(8, 256+1, 8)
L2norms_of_differences = np.zeros_like(ells, dtype=float)

for i, lmax in enumerate(ells):
    f_in = np.array([[f(theta, phi) - f_average
                      for phi in np.linspace(0.0, 2*np.pi, num=2*lmax+1, endpoint=False)]
                     for theta in np.linspace(0.0, np.pi, num=2*lmax+1, endpoint=True)])
    alm2 = spinsfast.map2salm(f_in, s, lmax)
    f_out = spinsfast.salm2map(alm2, s, lmax, 2*lmax+1, 2*lmax+1)
    
    difference_abs_squared_map = np.array(np.abs(f_in - f_out)**2, dtype=complex)
    difference_abs_squared_alm = spinsfast.map2salm(difference_abs_squared_map, 0, lmax)
    difference_abs_squared_alm[1:] = 0.0j
    L2norms_of_differences[i] = spinsfast.salm2map(difference_abs_squared_alm, 0, lmax, 2*lmax+1, 2*lmax+1)[0, 0].real

    print("lmax:", lmax,
          "\tMaximum deviation:", np.max(abs(f_in - f_out))/abs(np.max(f_in)),
          "\tL2 norm of deviation:", L2norms_of_differences[i])

plt.semilogy(ells, L2norms_of_differences)
plt.xlabel(r'$\ell_{\mathrm{max}}$')
plt.ylabel(r'$L^2$ norm of difference between input and output');
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1470769/28534434-b1a0abf8-706e-11e7-9887-1b3455e06745.png)
The convergence is very slow because the function is discontinuous, but it is converging.  It might be a little slower than exponential, but it still looks close to me.  So I would say that this looks right.

Hi Alex,

This depends on the granularity of your data. Following is based on the assumption that the time series is daily.

Then bi-weekly frequency should be 14 if you assume weekdays are different (i.e, Monday is different from Tuesday).  `seasonality(period=14)`. An example for this would be

Week 1: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7
Week 2: 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2
Week 3: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7
...

 If you assume there is no difference among weekdays (i.e., Monday is the same as Tuesday), then you should consider using longSeason `longSeason(period=2, stay=7, ...)`. An example for this would be

Week 1: 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1
Week 2: 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2
Week 3: 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1
...

For monthly frequency, it depends on what you mean by "monthly". If you mean the first day of each month should be the same, then you should use `seaonality(period=30)` or `seaonality(period=31)` depending on your belief of the month length... If you mean January this year should be the same as the January of next year, then you should use `longSeason(period=12, stay=30)` or  `longSeason(period=12, stay=31)`

Likewise for quarterly, depending on the actual meaning you want, you might choose either `seasonality(period=90)` or `longSeason(period=4, stay=90)`.

As my further investigation, I understand what tabula-java's current implementation is around `-a`.

In short, the option around area will be considered as following order (left is strong):
`-r` = `-l` > `-g` > `-a`

1. with `-r/--spreadsheet` or `-l/--lattice` option
   It works as [SPREADSHEET mode](https://github.com/tabulapdf/tabula-java/blob/master/src/main/java/technology/tabula/CommandLineApp.java#L227) and ignores both `-g` and `-a`. 

2. with `-g/--guess` and `-a/--area` option
  It works as [BASIC mode](https://github.com/tabulapdf/tabula-java/blob/master/src/main/java/technology/tabula/CommandLineApp.java#L232). In that mode, after setting [an area](https://github.com/tabulapdf/tabula-java/blob/master/src/main/java/technology/tabula/CommandLineApp.java#L48), it will be overriden by [guess mode](https://github.com/tabulapdf/tabula-java/blob/master/src/main/java/technology/tabula/CommandLineApp.java#L376).

3. with only `-a` option
  It works as BASIC mode and area option will not be overridden.

Anyway, I think it depends on the tabula-java's implementation, but  I will fix my document.

> In separation, are you using transparency?

Yes.  For each source, we generate a colormap that interpolates from (1,1,1,0) (white, transparent) to `(r,g,b,1)` where `(r,g,b)` is a color sourced from the axis property cycler.  The source is then normalized by the cumulative spectrogram peak (ie aggregating all sources and taking the max over time/frequency), and they are overlayed on top of each other.

We could alternately make it cycle up to `(r,g,b,0.75)` (or something) so that overlapping energy between sources adds up to a new color, but it might be confusing on a white background.  Maybe a better option is to do it starting from a black background?

After a lot of thinking I came up to the conclusion that it is not convenient to calculate these metrics into `memory_info_ex`. The main reason (other than for the big slowdown they introduce for a function which was previously very fast) is that inspecting the process address spaces implies having higher privileges. As such the "normal" metrics may succeed but PSS/USS may not. As such we set them to `0` (because we don't want to lose the other info). But by setting something to zero you have no way to figure whether it's `0` because it's the real value or because you had no enough privileges. In summary, to me this screams like something which requires a brand new, separate function. So I did in https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/pull/757. Now we have a new `Process.memory_addrspace_info()` returning USS, PSS and SWAP metrics on Linux and USS only on OSX and Windows which raises `AccessDenied` as expected. Also, I deprecated `memory_info_ex` in favour of `memory_info` which now returns all "fast" memory stats.

I understand the healthy impulse to avoid too much hand-crafting, but given that langid is a relatively static problem I see value in some sort of ultimate dataset against which a system can do a lookup for the top 10000 (ie top 90%) of queries.

1) I would perhaps just make some rules.  There are good libs and gists out there to match URLs, email addresses, numbers, emoji, mixed alphanumeric and other types of non-words.  Even if it doesn't catch all non-language, it will be better than now.  Another approach is to just return 'und' if no language is sufficiently probable nor more probable than others.  (I mean, what does it mean if a decently long sentence has no probability higher than 10%, split roughly evenly between 5 languages that are not related to each other?)

2) It can be done in a way where we get a performance boost even with just a list of stopwords for the top few languages.  For such lists, many have used NLTK (eg https://gist.github.com/bittlingmayer/ba17969070c2749b478f).  It has it's strengths but it's far from perfect, I would avoid relying on this approach as more than a confirmation, or otherwise penalising languages for which there are not stopwords.  (eg, we can use it as a tiebreaker between two languages for which we have a list - say en/de/es.)

3) I think punct is one of those things worth handcoding, although I agree the model should handle punct and special chars like any alpha char.  Regarding "¡No!" returning 'zh', I think we need to dig deeper.  It's likely a fundamental bug.

4) The issue (not unique to this lib of course) with *summative* models is that there are points awarded to a lang for having a char, but no concept of taking points away.  So let's say we have some long string "Xxxx Xxxxxxx xxxxx xx xxxxxx xx Xxxxx." (where X/x are real chars).  And we must decide between Swedish and Danish, for some set of words and characters that are very similar in both.  For the presence of 'ä', we could know it's not Danish, but under the current regime, in a long string, there is hardly any boost from that - it can easily get lost in the noise.

5) This I understand is a bit more of an undertaking.  The safest and easiest way I have seen it done is to just programmatically produce translit from proper text.  (Same as for producing Latin-alphabet languages like Spanish or German without accent marks.)

Re features, I guess the implicit goal is to compress it a bit to keep the library size small?  Because I would expect a string like "Jedenfalls empfehlenswert!" to be classified correctly every time.  (2 long words both unique to one big language.)  So we only back off to char n-grams if there is no match.

I have certainly found that training data are frequently contaminated.  This often occurs with false equations of country with language (eg .ba -> bs, although I must say langid.py performs well on that normally problematic set of languages).

Overall I hope you see that I am not suggesting to solve any of these issues 100%, but to incorporate a good-enough solution in such a way as to get some upside with no downside, cheaply.

It would be possible in theory, however I previously decided that the main process should not attempt to restart worker process if it dies.

Instead whatever scheduler or supervisor you're using for the main process should restart that.

For example I'm running arq with docker, in compose I have

```yaml
  worker:
    build: .
    entrypoint:
    - arq
    - path/to/worker.py
    restart: always
    depends_on:
    - redis
```

So docker is responsible for restarting the container, with that I get all the good of docker's restart logic: `docker ps` shows how long the worker has been running for and could abandon restarts if it never works.

If you really want `arq` to restart itself and aren't using docker or another scheduler you could create a very short bash script to do so.

```
#!/bin/bash

max=3
domain='example.com'
emca='/home/robot/.emca.conf'
ssl='/etc/ssl/public'
crt="$ssl/server.crt"
pat='\/\.well-known\/acme-challenge\/'

sed -i "/$pat/ s/^#//" /etc/nginx/server.conf
invoke-rc.d nginx reload

install -d -o robot -g www-data -m110 /srv/www/.well-known
install -d -o robot -g www-data -m310 /srv/www/.well-known/acme-challenge
sudo -u robot emca auth /home/robot/.emca.conf
sudo -u robot emca sign /home/robot/.emca.conf > "$crt".0; ret="$?"
rm -rf /srv/www/.well-known

if [[ "$ret" -ne 0 ]]
then rm "$crt".0
else
        for i in $(seq "$max" | tac); do
                if [[ -f "$crt"."$i" ]]; then
                        if [[ "$i" -eq "$max" ]]
                        then rm "$crt"."$i"
                        else mv "$crt"."$i" "$crt"."$((i + 1))"
                        fi
                fi
        done
        mv "$crt" "$crt".1
        mv "$crt".0 "$crt"
        cat "$crt" "$ssl"/chain.crt > "$ssl"/"$domain".crt.0
        mv "$ssl"/"$domain".crt.0 "$ssl"/"$domain".crt
fi

sed -i "/$pat/ s/^/#/" /etc/nginx/server.conf
invoke-rc.d nginx reload
```

I'm using version 1.0.1 and having the same issue - is there any workaround or fix?

You should always run `producer.close()` before exiting if you want to guarantee the message queue is flushed. If you want to manage exceptions, try / except / finally is always available.

Nonetheless, the producer does flush at exit (and at `__del__`), it's just that it does not block (much) so messages may get dropped. see https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/master/kafka/producer/kafka.py#L263-L267

Much preferred that you call close() directly and set an explicit timeout that you are comfortable with given the context of your application.

My use case is CAN bus messages.  They are considered an 8-byte array of data with values packed inside.  The two main higher-level protocols on CAN (CANopen and J1939) both use little endian for each packed value regardless of what processor they are run on.  There's no particular reason each signal can't individually be different endianness.

I think you want to be doing this just on the `npairs` or `weight` attribute.  Not the `NNCorrelation` object as a whole.

The point of the `+=` operation in treecorr is not for this kind of thing.  Rather it is to allow for distributing the processing across multiple machines.  The idea is that you'd run `process_auto` or `process_cross` on some subset of the data over multiple jobs so that in total, you've done everything.  But then you still need to add it all together into a single correlation object and then call `finalize` to finish the calculation.  These steps would be done as an afterburner on a single machine (presumably reading in the various results from files).

For `NNCorrelation`, `finalize` doesn't do a whole lot, so I can see how this wouldn't be particularly obvious in that case.  But for every other kind of correlation, it divides by the total weight in each bin.  (`NNCorrelation` also does that for `meanr` and `meanlogr`, but that's it.)

So I don't think `__isub__` or `__mul__` make sense given this intended usage of `__iadd__`.  Indeed, what would you want to do to the `meanr` and `meanlogr` arrays?  Subtracting or multiplying them don't make a whole lot of sense.  Rather, I think for doing jackknife calculations, you would only want to do these operations on the `npairs` (or `weight`) attribute, which is a numpy array that already has these operations defined.  For other kinds of correlations, you typically work with the `xi` or `xip`/`xim` attributes.

1. Nothing prohibits you from allowing the user to specify their own form of entropy. Just make the default: `uuid4().int`:
```python
def make_uid(entropy_src=None, prefix='2.25.'):
    if entropy_src is None:
        entropy_src = uuid4().int

    # Converet this to an int with the maximum available digits
    avail_digits = 64 - len(prefix)
    int_val = entropy_src % (10 ** avail_digits)
...
```
2. This is a major stretch:

> We have less chance of a collision (192 bits of entropy versus 128).

a. `random().hex() # 64-bit randomness`

[From the docs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/random.html):
>* It produces 53-bit precision floats
* being completely deterministic, it is not suitable for all purposes, and is completely unsuitable for cryptographic purposes.

b. How much entropy is there really here: `os.getpid()` ?

c. So you are ending up with a `sha256` of `uuid1`. All in, this seems to have less entropy than `uuid4`

3. Are you [confusing `urandom` with `random`](https://en.wikipedia.org/?title=/dev/random#Linux)?

[From the docs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/os.html#os.urandom) for `os.urandom`:
>The returned data should be unpredictable enough for cryptographic applications

If you are concerned about entropy count and `uuid` collisions, than it seems that we should be willing t to deal with what every slowness there is in `urandom` ("cryptographic applications" seems like a pretty important requirement).

Observe that all the previous libraries are linking to libodbc.so.*2* and libodbcinst.so.*2* but your pyODBC is linking to libodbc.so and libodbcinst.so (without the .2) . That's probably a very old version --- see what `ls -l /lib64/libodbc*` outputs on your machine... you have at least 2 different versions of unixODBC installed and pyODBC is linking to the wrong one.

The solution is to remove the old unixODBC (unless you have other applications which need it and would be broken by the newer versions) and pyODBC, then install unixODBC >= 2.3.1 and pyODBC.

Let's suppose `BusABC` has a detect method, like Giuseppe proposed. I would call it `detect_available_channels` since not all interfaced work with actual hardware. For example `virtual` or `socketcan`'s `vcan0` only work in software. It could look like this (notice that it is not abstract):

```Python
# in BusABC:
@staticmethod
def _detect_available_channels():
    """Docstring."""
    raise NotImplementedError()
```

Then I would define this method and make it accessible directly from the `can` module:

```Python
# in can/interface.py

from __future__ import absolute_import

import sys
import logging

from .interface import BACKENDS

log = logging.getLogger('can.interface')

if sys.version_info.major > 2:
    basestring = str

def detect_available_channels(search_only_in=None):
    """Dostring.

    :param search_only_in: either
        - the name of an interface as a string,
        - an iterable of interface names, or
        - `None` to search in all known interfaces.
    """

    if search_only_in is None:
        # use an iterator over the keys so we do not have to copy it
        search_only_in = BACKENDS.iterkeys()
    elif isinstance(search_only_in, basestring):
        search_only_in = (search_only_in,)
    # else it is an iterable of strings

    result = []
    for interface in search_only_in:

        # use the same code as in can/interface.py :: Bus.__new__()
        bus_class = get_class_of_this(interface)

        # get available channles
        try:
            result += bus_class._detect_available_channels()
        except NotImplementedError:
            log.debug('interface "%s" does not support detection of available channels', interface)

    return result
```

If you use intraday data directly, you can then operate close to the opening. If you have *minute* resolution bars you can operate 1 minute after the opening, and this should give you a good entry point close to the opening. You also operate in `next` as a rule of thumb.

`prenext` is called before all indicators are delivering meaningful data. In the simple case of a single moving average, `prenext` is called the n-1 periods before the average produces a data. After that `next` is called.

Data Replay needs smaller resolution data to give you larger resolution data. You can go from minutes to days. But you cannot go from day to day.

Hence the suggestion to simulate intraday data by breaking your daily bars into `open` + `high-low-close` bars and *replay* those 2 ticks.

Hi, I have actually "deprecated" DataResampler and DataReplayer in favor of the "filter" technology and the resampler and replayer filters.

The "filters" are described here: http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2015-11-21-data-filters/data-filling-filtering/

The resampling post was updated: http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2015-09-25-tickdata-resample/resample-tickdata/

Ways to resample/replay data:

  - Over Cerebro

    ```python
    cerebro.resampledata(data, **kwargs)  # kwargs -> same as the existing ones for DataResampler
    cerebro.replaydata(data. **kwargs)  #  # kwargs -> same as the existing ones for DataReplayer
    ```

  - Over the data itself (this is what ``cerebro`` internally does)

    ```python
    data.resample(**kwargs)
    cerebro.add(data, name=xx)  # name=None is the default
    ```

  - If you for example resample a daily data to weekly and want to use both simultaneously

    ```python
    cerebro.adddata(data)
    cerebro.resampledata(data.clone(), **kwargs)
    ```

I think the right way to check if a VCS tool is installed is as follow:

```python
def is_installed(cmd):
    """
    Check that a command is installed and its help message can be displayed.

    This function works well for "git", "hg" and "svn" if their ``bin`` path
    are in the PATH environment variable.

    :param cmd: Name of the command.
    :return: True if the command is installed, otherwise False.
    """
    try:
        args = [cmd, "help"]
        if hasattr(subprocess, "DEVNULL"):
            # Py3 has DEVNULL
            return subprocess.check_call(args,
                                         stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
                                         env=SUBPROCESS_ENV) or True
        else:
            from os import devnull

            with open(devnull, b"wb") as DEVNULL:
                return subprocess.check_call(args,
                                             stdout=DEVNULL,
                                             env=SUBPROCESS_ENV) or True
    except EnvironmentError:
        # command not found
        return False
```

Then, you can rewrite the xfail_if_no_* functions:

```python
xfail_if_no_git = pytest.mark.xfail(
    not is_installed("git"),
    reason="git is not installed"
)

xfail_if_no_hg = pytest.mark.xfail(
    not is_installed("hg"),
    reason="hg is not installed"
)
```

3. Javascript descriptors - variables can be not-writable or non-configurable etc + getters, setters

Digging a bit deeper, there are things in transactions that are really not documented at all, but the correct way to do what I wanted to do there is a bit counter-intuitive:

```
c = etcd3.client()
# The key doesn't exist, so its create_revision is not "> 0"
c.transaction(compare=[c.transactions.create('test') > 0], success=[], failure=[c.transactions.put('test', 'val')]]) # Returns (False, [None]), so the failure condition gets executed
assert c.get('test') == b'val'
# This time the test is successful, as the key has a created_version now
c.transaction(compare=[c.transactions.create('test') > 0], success=[], failure=[c.transactions.put('test', 'val1')])
assert c.get('test') == b'val'
```

So no need to add support for empty values as it seemed logical from the coreos docs.

we set use_fsync=True.  According to RocksDB docs,

http://pyrocksdb.readthedocs.io/en/v0.4/api/options.html#rocksdb.Options.use_fsync

this should be enough to guarantee on-disk consistency.

There is nothing else that ElectrumX can set or control here I think.  Perhaps RocksDB or the O/S and filesystem setup you have is part of the problem.

FWIW I've not yet had a report of this happening with LevelDB, but that may be chance.

Writing an examining key types with v1:

```
>>> db = sqlitedict.SqliteDict('./test.sqld', tablename='unicode')
>>> db[u"Řehůřek"] = 1
>>> for key in db:
....     print(repr(key))
....
'\xc5\x98eh\xc5\xaf\xc5\x99ek'

>>> db = sqlitedict.SqliteDict('./test.sqld', tablename='ints')
>>> db[1] = 1
>>> db[2] = 2
>>> for key in db:
....     print(repr(key))
....
'1'
'2'
```

In both cases, to pull the data from the database would require some code:

```
>>> db = sqlitedict.SqliteDict('./test.sqld', tablename='unicode')
>>> for key in (_key.decode('utf8') for _key in db):
....     print(repr(key))
u'\u0158eh\u016f\u0159ek'
>>> db = sqlitedict.SqliteDict('./test.sqld', tablename='ints')
>>> for key in (int(_key) for _key in db):
....     print(repr(key))
1
2
```

In the first case, upgrading to sqlitedict v2 would fail: the code continues to insert unicode, and decode it on its way out, but it is already unicode on its way out:

```
>>> u'\u0158eh\u016f\u0159ek'.decode('utf8')
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\u0158' in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)
```

In the second case, you are just lucky that int(int) == int :)

This is probably not easily fixed (or maybe something I do not want to). However if you add `n.reload()` right after you create it, it will work.

What is happening is that you are getting the movie definition from the URL `/library/sections/1/all` which only returns a partial subset of the items in the full XML details of the file (which would be a URL like `/library/metadata/999`). We try to be smart and load the full xml when an attribute is missing in the partial information in these lists, but in this case <Media> is actually there, just incomplete, so it got confused and didn't reload. So you need to force it.

You know, we could probably solve the PyPy perf problem by switching to maintaining our own stack rather than doing actual recursion. This would be a major project but an interesting one. It would also dodge the interpreter's recursion limits.

Line breaks are a kind of whitespace, and need to be handled in the grammar, usually as part of a regex rule. Look for ``\r`` (carriage return) and ``\n`` (new lines), which may appear alone or in combination, and collect or ignore them as needed in your grammar.

For an example, see the parsimonious grammar used to parse grammars:

https://github.com/erikrose/parsimonious/blob/master/parsimonious/grammar.py#L260-L263

I think we will implement the simplified equivalent of `\X` that some engines have used in the past.  It's better than nothing I think as I imagine people found the simplified form in engines of the past useful before the more advanced form was made available.

So thinking about this, we should hopefully be able to handle pre-processing the search pattern once (at most twice since after finding important flags, we may have to re-walk the pattern).

Ultimately, this is what should have been done the first time had I not been so lazy.  Ironically, it probably wouldn't have been more work the first time, just different work. We still don't have to tokenize with perfect understanding of every regex/re feature, we just need to be less naive.  We only care about:

- Various character properties and if they are in a group, quote (`Q...E`), or comment.
- Where the groups start and end
- Where verbose comments start and end
- Where inline comments start and end `(?#)`
- And where `\Q...\E` start and end.

There just isn't an easy way to do this without recursively walking the string and just rebuilding it as we want in the first pass.  We need to be aware of the interactions of all these various behaviors.

- Actually won't rebuild the pattern live as we may rewalk it when we discover that `verbose` is enabled, or `V1` of regex is enabled, or the `unicode`  or `ascii` flag has changed how we should process things.
- We can keep a an array of modifications such as `Q...E`, `(?#)`, `# verbose`, and various property replacements, which include the region in which they will replace.  Then at the end we can simply split the pattern on those boundaries rebuilding the pattern with the modifications.
- Once we see all three flags we need to track, we "could" abort the first walkthrough, and restart with new logic if needed. If none of the flags actually require us to stop, we can just continue.
- Shouldn't have to track search groups per se since we will now be dropping into them via recursive functions and making all of our  "group" based decisions at that time.

This should get us to a better a place moving forward.

@dkg made a good argument to me today, to *not* include the wordlist as a nameplate attribute. The problem is that an attacker can scan those attributes before committing to a particular channel, so they could selectively break connections using a given language, while leaving other connections untouched.

As annoying as the UX and engineering consequences are, it's probably better for overall security to prevent attackers from being selective about their attacks: force them to attack (or not) all connections with equal probability, rather than letting them pick and choose their victims. That means either putting the wordlist-identifier as a *mailbox* attribute (which clients can't learn until they've claimed the nameplate, at which point they're the only one who can use it, so either they get the code right or it is broken for everybody), or not using a wordlist at all.

He suggested an interesting approach to L10N. Basically you pick half a dozen *writing systems*, say (latin, cyrillic, greek, arabic, chinese, tamil), for each one you pick the most common language written with those characters, and then you find 256 words from each. The sender's code is chosen from a subset according to their language settings (`$LANG=en_US` etc). The receiver uses the whole wordlist, but once the user has typed in the first character of a word, any tab-completion is only going to show them words from the same language. So you can effectively hide all the other words from any individual recipient, without needing any in-band indicator of the wordlist being used.

The downside is that you can't localize for specific languages: (english, french, italian, spanish) are all going to get the same wordlist. But it doesn't preclude us from adding an in-band wordlist attribute later, and certainly wouldn't add any information to enable selective attacks in the meantime.

I created this version in my attempt to figure this all out.  This version uses readchar's constants from keys.py and functions as expected.

```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
from __future__ import print_function

import readchar


class _Getch:
    """Gets a single character from standard input.  Does not echo to the
screen."""
    def __init__(self):
        try:
            self.impl = _GetchWindows()
        except ImportError:
            self.impl = _GetchUnix()

    def __call__(self): return self.impl()


class _GetchUnix:
    def __init__(self):
        pass
        # import tty, sys

    def __call__(self):
        import sys
        import tty
        import termios
        fd = sys.stdin.fileno()
        old_settings = termios.tcgetattr(fd)
        try:
            tty.setraw(sys.stdin.fileno())
            ch = sys.stdin.read(1)
        finally:
            termios.tcsetattr(fd, termios.TCSADRAIN, old_settings)
        return ch


class _GetchWindows:
    def __init__(self):
        # noinspection PyUnresolvedReferences
        import msvcrt

    def __call__(self):
        # noinspection PyUnresolvedReferences
        import msvcrt
        return msvcrt.getch()

g = _Getch()
running = True
while running:
    x = readchar.key.CTRL_C
    charbuffer = b""
    while True:
        char1 = bytes(g.__call__())

        if char1 == x:
            print("it was a ctrl-c")
            running = False
            break

        if (charbuffer + char1) not in readchar.key.ESCAPE_SEQUENCES:
            charbuffer += (bytes(char1))
            print("!")
            break
        charbuffer += (bytes(char1))

        print(".", end="")

    # print("'%s'" % bytes(charbuffer))

    if charbuffer == readchar.key.UP:
        print("YESSSSSS...")
    print(charbuffer)
```
As you can see the logic is slightly different in this version because readchar reads a character first, then enters the while loop (calling more instances of itself to get more characters!), whereas this version just does all the reads in the while loop.  For some reason, readchar does not appear to add the final byte to make the complete escape sequence.. at least, that is what it seems to be.

It turns out that "enum types" cannot be referred to by $ref in Swagger 2.0.

Instead, I have to use the tag.  For example, this type:

```
 AlarmClass:

   enum: &AlarmClassEnum
   - Network
   - Service
   - Storage
   - System
```

Has a tag &AlarmClassEnum.  When I want to define a property of this type, I have to do this:

```
      class:
        type: string
        enum: *AlarmClassEnum
```

Switching to this makes 'class' a string, which works.

I think you can write a scanner to collect all those IDs on you own, the __app.op__ is a little bit more complicated than what you need (to be compatible with Swagger 1.2).

Here is a pseudo code(not tested) to collect all Operation objects in pyswagger, similar to what we did in __pyswagger.scanner.TypeReduce__:
```python
from pyswagger import SwaggerApp
from pyswagger.scan import Scanner, Dispatcher
from pyswagger.spec.v2_0.objects import Operation

# define your scanner
class OperationCollector(object):
    class Disp(Dispatcher): pass

    def __init__(self):
        self.cached_obj = []

    @Disp.register([Operation])
    def _op(self, path, obj, _):
        self.cached_obj.append(obj)


# load your definition
app = SwaggerApp.create("path_to_your_definition")
s = Scanner(app)
coll = OperationCollector()
s.scan(root=app.root, route=[coll])

# access all Operation objects, no matter with IDs or not
for v in coll.cached_obj:
    pass
```

As far as calibrated probabilities are concerned, i keep it in mind but i use the train data as test data as well (in some cases) and it is mentioned that the data for calibrated probabilities should not be same as train data. Instead i used a combination of the precision-recall example from sklearn [here](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/auto_examples/model_selection/plot_precision_recall.html) and code from scikit-plot to make a precision-recall curve based on `decision_function` method. trash-empty should empty all your trashed files, it means that it will delete trashed files from these directories: 
- the `~/.local/share/Trash` directory
- all the `$topdir/.Trash/$uid` directories 
- all the `$topdir/.Trash-$uid` directories 

For example if you had uid=1000, in your case it should remove at least all your trashed files from:
- `~/.local/share/Trash` directory
- `/media/d/.Trash/1000`
- `/media/d/.Trash-1000`

For example it does not remove files from other user directories (for example if your uid is 1000 it will not touch any file in  `/media/d/.Trash-1001` nor any file in `/media/d/.Trash/1001`).

It will remove only files that represent 'garbage' it will remove only these files:
- .trashinfo files (for example /media/d/.Trash/1000/info/*.trashinfo`)
- backup copy files (for example /media/d/.Trash/1000/files/*`)
it will leave untouched any file that does not match one of these two categories.

Please provide more information about the non deleted contents of your trash directories including full file names, permissions and mount type of your partition if you want reopen this issue. Also check if you have the latest version of `trash-cli`.

```diff
import os
```

```python
    training_face_encoding.append(face_recognition.face_encodings(training_image)[0])

face_locations = []
face_encodings = []
face_names = []
process_this_frame = True
i = 0
while True:
    ret, frame = video_capture.read()
    frame=cv2.flip(frame, 1)
    small_frame = cv2.resize(frame, (0, 0), fx=0.25, fy=0.25)

    if i < 4:
        process_this_frame=False
        i += 1
    else:
        i = 0
    if process_this_frame:
        face_locations = face_recognition.face_locations(small_frame)
        face_encodings = face_recognition.face_encodings(small_frame, face_locations)

        face_names = []
        for face_encoding in face_encodings:
            match = face_recognition.compare_faces(training_face_encoding, face_encoding)
            name = "Unknown"
            print(match)
            print(files)

            i = 0
            for m in match:
                if m:
                    name = files[i]
                i += 1

            face_names.append(name)

    process_this_frame = not process_this_frame

    for (top, right, bottom, left), name in zip(face_locations, face_names):
        top *= 4
        right *= 4
        bottom *= 4
        left *= 4

        cv2.rectangle(frame, (left, top), (right, bottom), (0, 0, 255), 2)

        cv2.rectangle(frame, (left, bottom - 35), (right, bottom), (0, 0, 255), cv2.FILLED)
        font = cv2.FONT_HERSHEY_DUPLEX
        cv2.putText(frame, name, (left + 6, bottom - 6), font, 1.0, (255, 255, 255), 1)

    cv2.imshow('Video', frame)

    if cv2.waitKey(1) & 0xFF == ord('q'):
        break

video_capture.release()
cv2.destroyAllWindows()
```

Basically all i changed was to add the required methods to HMCover.
The decision weather it is titlable or not takes place in the `_init_data_struct` method.

```python
    def _init_data_struct(self):
        """Generate a data dictoinary (self._data) from metadata."""
        self._state = "LEVEL"
        self._data.update({self._state: STATE_UNKNOWN})
        try:
            self._data.update({'LEVEL_2': self._hmdevice.get_cover_tilt_position()})
        except:
            pass
```

Home Assistant decides whether a cover is tiltable or not by getting the property `current_cover_tilt_position` and there i test if LEVEL_2 is in self._data. When it is not, None is returned and Home Assistant assumes that the cover is not tiltable. Otherwise the tilt value is returned and Home Assistant shows the appropriate controls and uses HMCover's cover_tilt methods.

> `LIBUSB_ERROR_ACCESS [-3]` means Access denied (insufficient permissions).

Correct.

> Do I need to set any kinds of permission in my system before I can use this package?

Yes. The way I personally do is using udev rules to tag the device with `uaccess` so that current session can access the device:
```
$ cat /etc/udev/rules.d/60-edison.rules
ACTION=="add", SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ATTR{idVendor}=="8087", ATTR{idProduct}=="0a99", TAG+="uaccess"
```
Or I directly set device's group to `plugdev` and make the user member of that group (old fashion). In the case of this device I have an extra rule to trigger a firmware load when default device id appears, and then it reappears as another `idProduct` and I finally grant access, but I could just as well have used `uaccess` here:
```
$ cat /etc/udev/rules.d/ITI1480A.rules
ACTION!="add", GOTO="ITI_rules_end"
SUBSYSTEM!="usb", GOTO="ITI_rules_end"
ATTR{idVendor}!="16c0", GOTO="ITI_rules_end"

ATTR{idProduct}=="07a8", GROUP="plugdev"
ATTR{idProduct}=="07a8", RUN+="ITI1480A.sh '$attr{busnum}' '$attr{devnum}' '/lib/firmware/ITI1480A.ihx'"
ATTR{idProduct}=="07a9", SYMLINK="ITI1480A%n", GROUP="plugdev"

LABEL="ITI_rules_end"
```

> Also, which value should `INTERFACE` hold in the following code?

This depends on the device you want to control. Typically, there is a single interface, numbered 0, like in the following case (`bInterfaceNumber`):
```
$ lsusb -vd 1d6b:0002

Bus 001 Device 001: ID 1d6b:0002 Linux Foundation 2.0 root hub
Couldn't open device, some information will be missing
Device Descriptor:
[...]
  Configuration Descriptor:
[...]
    Interface Descriptor:
      bLength                 9
      bDescriptorType         4
      bInterfaceNumber        0
      bAlternateSetting       0
      bNumEndpoints           1
      bInterfaceClass         9 Hub
      bInterfaceSubClass      0 Unused
      bInterfaceProtocol      0 Full speed (or root) hub
      iInterface              0
      Endpoint Descriptor:
[...]
```

My reading is that this describes how python itself raises such error (when import statement on its own cannot succeed) rather than prescribing how to use this exception class.

> *I'm not doing anything yet.*

The alternative would be to postpone linker invocation: do not look for libusb1 at import time, but when USBContext is instanciated for the first time for example.

Which is an idea which already somewhat surfaced when I've been asked how to customise library lookup path. My answer at that time was to just do the same as one would do with any non-python program: use whatever linker configuration knobs available on considered platform (`LD_LIBRARY_PATH` and equivalents). This would also break backward compatibility (as error source would move from import time to somewhere later), but this feels like it brings something (errors can be handled in a more convenient place than around import statements) instead of just removing a distinction of why `python-libusb1` cannot work.

> has two different things that can go wrong when importing. And that I need to code against both of them separately.

My intention was precisely that it made possible to tell your users about two different solutions for two different problems, instead of stuffing them into one umbrella exception.

While python3 improved things a lot with `raise ... from ...`, `ImportError` can be very misleading in my experience: maybe the module being import is not there, or maybe it is another module imported inside that is not, or maybe it is an import cycle and everything is actually present, or maybe it is some python code actively raising it for whatever other reason.

Then again, handling errors at import time is not the most convenient place, as it then encourages importing elsewhere than module toplevel beginning (I'm thinking of GUI programs, which would need to display an error dialog telling users to install libusb1).

I'll keep thinking on this to find a better way, any API idea to postpone and customise library lookup is welcome.

> maybe with referencing the context locking behaviour in some documentation out there, like in the `hotplug.py` on the repo for example.

I agree. I added a mention on `usb1.USBContext.hotplugRegisterCallback` docstring, plus documented more explicitly the few methods on USBDevice which issue synchronous calls, plus comments in both hotplug examples: 896d600d0daedbd7cd48d187da59ee8158a5713f .

I guess the same limitation also applies during "normal" (non-hotplug) even handling, ie async API callbacks, but issuing a synchronous call inside async handler is less likely to happen and more likely to be quickly identified as problematic, so I think I do not need to document this limitation.

yeah, `GetCommandLineW` is ineffective because the command line arguments that are passed from the console to the launcher are channelled through the "narrow" `main(int argc, char **argv)`, instead of the "wide" (Windows specific) `wmain` entry point.

The distlib launchers use the same `GetCommandLineW` to access the arguments, before passing them to the wrapped script. The `argv` from `main` are discarded (I think they didn't use `wmain` because some mingw version didn't support it).

I still don't precisely know under what conditions it doesn't work for you. I only know it doesn't work in IDLE where it shouldn't work. When there is no stdio, `GetConsoleMode` should raise invalid handle, `streams.STDOUT.should_be_fixed()` should return False and `streams.stdout_text_transcoded` shouldn't be used at all.

No.

I must emphasize, that this is a Stateless system. It is designed for use with single page applications and mobile apps.

The LoginView is the POST endpoint for the user's credentials (e.g. username and password). The page that the user enters these into can be accessed without any form of login, as it should be static content.

When these credentials are authenticated by the auth system (e.g. BasicAuth), the LoginView returns a token that can be used as the auth from that device for all other views.

Removing the permission from LoginView would allow anyone to aquire an AuthToken whether they had authenticated or not.

If this is still unclear let me know. Please do not remove authentication from loginview in your apps though, I'd hate for security problems to happen due to a misunderstanding.

```
from mailchimp3 import MailChimp

client.lists.interest_categories.all(mailing_list['id'])

>>> AttributeError: 'Lists' object has no attribute '_get'
```

Interestingly, babel doesn't work that way (I discovered by digging into
the code). When it goes looking for its modules, it starts with the path of
the source file it has been asked to
transpile, and works up from there, looking for the node_modules directory.
(I have been told by someone who knows much more about node.js than I do
that that is intentional, to insure the environment doesn't change between
dev and release).

If you have node_modules somewhere else, try setting NODE_PATH directory,
> like env NODE_PATH=/directory/where/node_modules/is/located ./manage.py
> compilestatic. Or you can just install them globally with npm -g option.

Hi @MelissaLAC,

Thank you for your comment. You are right, we are using exactly the opposite notation from Steck for two forms of reduced dipole matrix elements (one being symmetric upon change of order of j1 and j2, and the other one not). There is unfortunately no consistent notation on this in the literature if you look at angular momentum algebra books from different authors. That's why [Atom calculator](https://atomcalc.jqc.org.uk) returns numerical values for both so that people can easily spot if their preferred notation matches with our or not.

We've chosen to use < j1 || ... || j2 > for the reduced d.m.e. that has the same value when you change order of j1 and j2 because bra ket notation looks like you are taking matrix element, and matrix elements should be just one complex conjugate different. This reserves ( j1 || ... || j2 ) notation for reduced d.m.e whose numerical value is not equal to ( j2 || ... || j1 ) in general. This is opposite to Steck notation.

There is a note on this in [ARC documentation](http://arc-alkali-rydberg-calculator.readthedocs.io/en/latest/alkali_atom_functions.html#arc.alkali_atom_functions.AlkaliAtom.getReducedMatrixElementJ_asymmetric) but it is good to have this noted here too as it often causes confusion. Thank you for your question and please let me know if something is unclear!

For your project, we have some [ARC black body examples here](http://arc-alkali-rydberg-calculator.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_static/Rydberg_atoms_a_primer.html#Rydberg-Atom-Lifetimes). Good luck!

Best wishes, Nikola

If you don't pass any parameter to `fetchmany()`, the default number of rows to be fetched is `cursor.arraysize = 1`. You can specify the `size` parameter to the number of rows to fetch per call.

Here are some solutions:
* Use `fetchall()` to fetch all rows
* Call `fetchmany(size=N)` to fetch N rows
* Change `cursor.arraysize` attribute to M, then every time calling `fetchmany()` would fetch M rows

Note: The fetchmany method try to fetch as many rows as indicated by the size parameter. If this is not possible due to the specified number of rows not being available, fewer rows may be returned.

This shouldn't be considered as hack. The proposed changes work under the following constraints:
1. the parameters of a block/hybridblock should always be able to be saved and loaded.
2. our block/hybridblock supports setattr with other block/hybridblocks.
3. when doing `net2.block1 = net1.block1`, `net1` and `net2` should continue to function normally.

Currently, the problem is that for 3., if `net1` and `net2` don't have the same prefix, 1. is violated. I don't think this is "unintended use case" since this is allowed under current design. I don't believe that forcing users to always do `with net2.name_scope(): create_net1()` will be productive, nor will it be easy to use.

This is because the forward loop doesn't wait for all computation to finish. ndarray is executed asynchronously. To address it, add `nd.waitall()` after `out = net(test)`. A more in-depth analysis written by @piiswrong about the cause of the issue and possible solutions: *Background* Gluon provides `save_params` API, which saves the model parameters (but not model definition) as a binary file ‘xxx.params’. It can be loaded back with API `load_params`. The saved file is intended to be opaque but you can load it with `mx.nd.load` and see the internal content (we don’t advertise this). Gluon also provides `export` API for saving a gluon model definition (.json) and parameters (.params), which can be loaded with MXNet Module or other language bindings. Gluon provides `SymbolBlock` API that can load .json model definition and .params parameter file. But an `import` helper for this functionality is missing. *The change* We changed the internal structure of the `.params` file saved by `save_params` to resolve a bug. Parameters saved by previous versions can still be loaded in new version. *The complaint from user* A user saved model definition and model parameters with `mx.sym.save_json` and `save_params` following the straight dope book. Because the book doesn’t show how to load it back, customer invented a hacky way to load it into SymbolBlock with load_params. User's code broke after upgrading from 1.1 to 1.2. *The cause* Customer’s hack depended on internal similarities between .params files saved by `save_params` and `export`. After the change of `save_params` format, this hack stopped working. *Faults on our part* - The straight dope book should have recommended saving model definition with `export` instead of `mx.sym.save_json` and `save_params`. - We should have provided an `import` utility so that user doesn’t need to invent their own hacks to load model definition into SymbolBlock. - (?) We changed the file format saved by `save_params`. Although it is intended as an opaque binary whose format is not defined in documentation, some customers could be depending on undefined behavior. *Solutions* 1. Revert `save_params` to previous format. Add new API `save_parameters` for new format. **Pros** - Customer relying on internal format of `save_params` won’t see breakage. **Cons** - All users need to manually migrate to new API `save_parameters` - Having both `save_params` and `save_parameters` could be confusing.

2. Issue warnings and error messages to instruct users to move to `export` and `import`, and stop depending on undefined behavior **Pros** - Most users won’t see breakage and won’t need to do anything. **Cons** - Users depending on `save_params`’s internal format will see breakage. For both solutions we can add more documentation and helper API to minimize impact. Current open PRs related to this issue: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/11236 https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/11127 https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/11210

It seems the error does not stop any functionality after a deeper look today.  It is likely that the original poster has not made user the "organization" ModelAdmin object derive from `ForeignKeyAutocompleteAdminMixin` (because this registers the admin autocomplete URL for that specific model).

So when that fails to reach the `foreignkey_autocomplete` URL it actually pattern matches to `^organization/<val>` and that means the key value is incorrect being `foreignkey_autocomplete`. For me this causes the requests to bounce out to the main admin page.

It was not obvious at first that if you want two objects connected with a foreignkey using autocomplete both have to derive from `ForeignKeyAutocompleteAdminMixin` and one has to implement `related_search_fields` which has the autocomplete field.

```
"""Example using zmq with asyncio coroutines"""
# Copyright (c) PyZMQ Developers.
# This example is in the public domain (CC-0)

import time

import zmq
from zmq.asyncio import Context, Poller, ZMQEventLoop
import asyncio

url = 'tcp://127.0.0.1:5555'

loop = ZMQEventLoop()
asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

ctx = Context()

@asyncio.coroutine
def ping():
    """print dots to indicate idleness"""
    while True:
        yield from asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        print('.')

@asyncio.coroutine
def receiver():
    """receive messages with polling"""
    pull = ctx.socket(zmq.REP)
    pull.connect(url)
    poller = Poller()
    poller.register(pull, zmq.POLLIN)
    while True:
        events = yield from poller.poll()
        if pull in dict(events):
            print("recving", events)
            msg = yield from pull.recv_multipart()
            print('recvd', msg)

@asyncio.coroutine
def sender():
    """send a message every second"""
    tic = time.time()
    push = ctx.socket(zmq.REQ)
    push.bind(url)
    while True:
        print("sending")
        yield from push.send_multipart([str(time.time() - tic).encode('ascii')])
        yield from asyncio.sleep(1)

loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait([
    ping(),
    receiver(),
    sender(),
]))

```

@autrilla yes, it does.

I've digged into this a little, and it seems the issue is with golang's `crypto/openpgp` encrypting.

In all tests sops encodes through `crypto/openpgp` succesfully, then fails to decode through `crypto/openpgp` with `Reading PGP message failed: openpgp: incorrect key` and fallbacks to binary gpg.

I've found an issue https://github.com/golang/go/issues/13409, which says that GPG treats input data as binary data, and golang's `crypto/openpgp` treats input data as text data.

Fix is fairly simple, I'll make a PR in a few minutes.

I haven't closed this issue because I wonder if there's a good way to make this work.

The recent changes in this area were to support python's pickle protocol.  Perhaps what's happening is that since the built-in exception classes implement it, then your classes are now getting caught by that handling.

There's the custom handler approach, which is a little too much effort for most users, and then there's the possibility that we could expose the old method as a built-in custom handler that you can use for these classes.

I hate breaking backwards compatibility too. That stings and sorry for doing that. We should probably add tests for this use case.

There are a couple of ways we can handle this, unfortunately none of them are backwards-compatible, but I'm open to suggestions.

One option is to expose a custom handler so that users can register their class with that handler.  The old method can be provided as a built-in handler so that users can easily register their classes with it.

Another option is to expose a registration method that sets the jsonpickle protocol version (here we'd set it to v1)  for a class and then use that as the basis for fixing #4 .  The appeal of this option is that it allows for future evolution and helps avoid future breakage, but it means we have to keep various permutations of the code around purely for compat purposes, which is a maintenance burden. It would also be a more involved rework of the code, which is why that issue's been on the backburner for so long.

OTOH, class-level overrides of the pickle protocol sounds kinda scary to implement, and is the same amount of effort for the user (one-liner to register the class), and obviously I like deferring that old issue as long as possible, so exposing a custom handler might be the simpler solution for now.

Do any of these options sound good?

The hard part about is that we're trying to follow the pickle protocol spec which means we run into shortcomings like this where following the spec gives suboptimal behavior for some use cases.  We can't fix past mistakes but hopefully we can provide enough hooks and batteries included to make common cases easy to handle.

The detail about calling `super()` with/without props is an interesting note. That really hints that the python designers expect things to be used in a specific way, which has merits, but ultimately the library should be pragmatic and flexible.  I would have never stumbled upon that fix, so in the very least we should add a note about exceptions in our documentation to help others who may run into this same issue.

Users that can modify their classes may choose to change how they call the superclass's `__init__()` once they learn about this distinction.  Users that cannot modify their classes (maybe they're generated by a tool, or third-party) can possibly use a built-in handler.

2 basic options: register a specific handler with jsonpickle, or use
`__reduce__` or `__reduce_ex__` to manage the serialisation.

>  I was looking for django field type to store EDTF data. And more that, the first question here is how can I deal with EDTF in django models and django admin forms by extension? I already test python-edft in django and I got some exceptions and strange behaviors (incomprehensible ways of deal). Maybe this implementation could be more documented (I could do that, but before I would like to understand). Is there a simple way form to input extended data (level 1 and 2) by django admin and show that in a template? Maybe a better example (a more explicit one) could be useful.

The API looks reasonable, but it's not totally clear to me *when* an app should be setting `backoff`'s logger in this manner. For example, imagine the following app layout:

```
tmp$ tree
.
├── app.py
├── blueprints
│   └── api.py
└── lib
    ├── bar.py
    └── foo.py
```

`app.py` contains all the Flask init code. It is where we would also configure logging using `logging.config.dictConfig`.

Each module has a module-level `get_logger` call, right at the beginning:

```python
from app import get_logger
logger = get_logger(__name__)
...
```

So in this example, if both `api.py` and `lib/foo.py` import `backoff` and want to use it, when is it appropriate to call `backoff.set_logger()`? It seems a bit weird for `app.py` to import it and configure it at log configuration time, especially if `app.py` never makes an explicit call using `backoff`.

Anyway, perhaps I am just very confused on this topic, so in the interest of not blocking progress, I would say that providing that API makes sense, and I think app developers can make it all work. :)

Yes, being concerned about involved costs to clients is important.

As the buckets do not move across regions often (one has to delete it and after some time create in another region - rather unusual case), I would think of:

- having a cache, which maps existing buckets to regions.
- if region could not be detected from initial bucket listing - the cash would be used to guess the region
- if the call to region known from the cache would fail, then make the special call to find it explicitly.

The problem we talk about is, that someone must take care of the information, which region is particular bucket using. The options are:

1. let the client do all the work.
2. do all this work by `boto3` and do not care about additional calls to the services
3. do it in `boto3` and try to optimize as far as possible.

Currently we are using option 1 and I have to say, it is not satisfactory for few reasons (e.g. clients must repetitively write a code which they are not much good at).

Option 2 sounds to me as plain improvement and step 3 is is natural optimization after that.

I would expect, that well written option 2 (and for sure option 3) would result in overall savings of costs for most of applications (better quality solution to common problem being tested by many users compared to bunch of home made fixtures).

I perceive at the moment as the biggest problem that current API does not follow concepts which many users intuitively have about what is AWS S3 bucket, objects stored on them and how to use them.

Another idea: At the moment user gets the answer "talk to another endpoint" - I guess the response is already redirecting somewhere. Can we use this redirection as source for region_name to use?

This is on VMware vCenter Server Appliance 6.0.0.0 (6.0.0.0 Build 4811166).

I've seen this before... most notably back when I was working on the OpenStack Nova driver for vSphere. The problem is *ambiguity* ... the placement task doesn't know where to place the VM. In many situations there's only one *obvious* location to drop in the new VM and vCenter will just fill in the blank for you. The interesting thing is that even though the API specifies Folder ... I think the concept of resource pool is actually more *fundamental* and that's because a folder, host, or other containing structure may hold *multiple* resource pools.

By default, there's a *hidden* implicit resource pool inside each container-like thing. A host is a resource pool, a folder is sort of a resource pool. Leave out the resource pool and specify a folder/host and as long as there's no ambiguity the vCenter Server will figure out what you mean and place the VM in the implicit resource pool representing the entire Folder or Host (at least that's the behavior we saw).

However, if you have a multi-host or multi resource pool environment underneath the location you specified things can be ambiguous. Which pool did you mean? vCenter can't read your mind so it fails. You didn't post details about your resource pool configuration but I'm guessing you have multiple resource pools and multiple hosts ... and possibly multiple datacenters. This is the situation in which vCenter can't decide for you and at the API level it probably *shouldn't*.

Now, I've not looked inside `vCLI` but my guess is that it probably makes the same API call made here but instead of leaving the pool blank, the vCLI probably quietly looks up the best fit resource pool for you so you don't have to figure it out. That's a good end-user tool behavior. It's a behavior `pyVmomi` just doesn't have.

The `pyVmomi` library isn't an end-user tool. This is an integration library, API language binding, or client library as you may alternatively call it. As such, (in my opinion), it's not our place to silently do things that may or may not solve problems. In this case the API is functioning as designed. It would make sense to enhance these behaviors in [pyvmomi-tools](https://github.com/vmware/pyvmomi-tools) (which is still unreleased... but we have grand plans... *grand* plans...) or demonstrate an enhancement in [pyvmomi community samples](https://github.com/vmware/pyvmomi-community-samples).

Given enough prodding I might change my position on this, but this isn't a bug, it's a description of a missing feature. If you want to have ... what should we call it ... *implicit resource pool lookup* ... then feel free to request it as a feature. It's just not a bug. The `pyVmomi` library is faithfully rendering the API under it (even if we don't like the behavior).

I hope that helps. And, for the record, I wouldn't mind helping the community roll out a pyCLI or something built on top of pyVmomi that had many of the same behaviors people expect from a CLI tool as long as there was enough support for the project in terms of volunteer developers.

[RFC 8259](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc8259) says:

```
unescaped = %x20-21 / %x23-5B / %x5D-10FFFF
```

[ECMA-404](http://www.ecma-international.org/publications/files/ECMA-ST/ECMA-404.pdf) says:

> All code points may be placed within the quotation marks except for the code points that must be escaped: quotation mark (U+0022), reverse solidus (U+005C), and the control characters U+0000 to U+001F

Both standards therefore allow U+2028 and U+2029 to be left unescaped in JSON.

The change in simplejson 2.2.0 appears to have been made to ensure that we produce JSON that is valid Javascript (ie, can be used in JSONP), which is a reasonable goal, but I find it regrettable it was done without putting it behind an option flag, because as @serhiy-storchaka says, reversing the change does risk breaking existing code.

On the other hand, I still feel like the inconsistency with `json` is the bigger risk here.

Anyway, a reasonable compromise might be to put this behaviour behind an option flag along the lines of `ensure_ascii`, with it defaulting to the current behaviour?

The data is composed of tagged values which are each:
U16 Tag length (length of tagged data following these 2 bytes)
U8 Tag
Tag Length-1 bytes of tagged data
