`s = Summoner(id=123, region="NA")` does not make a call. A call is only made when data that is unavailable is requested. So if you run `s.account.id` it's going to make a call because `s`'s account id is unavailable. However, `s.id` _will not_ make a call because the `id` _is_ available.

Just pass in the account information when you create the summoner (and don't call `.load()` on it), then pass the summoner to the match history method. (The match history method needs the summoner's account id, not it's id.)

We've run into similar recursion errors quite a bit in the past, and have talked a lot about the underlying cause. We finally decided to just simplify some functionality in Cass to make these types of issues go away. I updated my code locally a few days ago and have been using it quite a bit to test it, and I believe it's all working. I just pushed a minute ago.

That commit comes with a boatload of other changes -- but the two you will need to worry about are the `Patch` and `Queue` changes. `Patch` now requires a region in it's constructors, so you'll have to add that parameter if you aren't using `cass.set_default_region`. The enum `Queue` names have also changed to be consistent with Riot's renaming, and since that broke some of our previous names of queues, we just renamed them all (they are so much easier to understand now).

All of the other changes should be invisible. In addition, you can remove the `UnloadedGhostStore()` from your data pipeline, because we now insert it automatically if it isn't there.

Hopefully these backend/invisible changes clean up all of the recursion (and similar) errors that I've seen too much of. We are continuing to better define the lines between all the different components in Cass, and that's what these backend changes have been done.

So tldr; repull from github and these errors should go away. You also might have to update your usage of `Queue` and `Patch`.

Hello @Jiyun-Cui,
When deprojecting pixel to 3d coordinate note that you're using a hard-coded value of 1 (meter) which is the (presumed) Z value of the specific pixel in the source frame.
> rs2_deproject_pixel_to_point(color_face_landmark_point[j], &color_intrin, color_face_landmark_pixel[j], **1**);


When projecting a depth pixel (which has actual Z) onto a 2D frame, the projection work as designed as the Z allows to produce to calculate the exact  [x,y,z] coordinates.
In this case the projection is between RGB frame and ir frame (two 2D sources), and since the RGBframe doesn't have depth data, you're filling the missing Z value in a function call with a "rough" estimate of where the pixel "lies" in the 3D space (1 meter), which produces the error.

If the target IR frame is from the left imager (i.e. has the same origin as depth frame), then 
you may run a simple test and check the calculate error will reach minimum for pixels that actually are at  1 meter, and then start growing again.

Hi @PulpQE/quality-engineers where do we stand on this issue?

Ah, yes. Pulp is only supported in a limited number of environments, and we don't care about other platforms or interesting user configurations, by it or other applications, presumably now or in the future. *sigh*

By hard-coding CLI SSH options into our application, we are creating an application that is unable to work in a variety of environments. One is where the target system disallows connection pooling. Another is where we're working with an older version of OpenSSH. I expect there are more cases. We already disallow the use of other SSH clients, such as Dropbear and SecureCRT. :/

To be clear, I am sympathetic to the complaint at the top of this thread. I would like for SSH connections to be efficient, through a mechanism such as connection pooling. But adopting a solution that further lowers the adaptability and portability of Pulp Smash is distasteful to me. This is especially true given that:

* Many circumstances already require users to edit their `~/.ssh/config`, and the solution of adding three lines to this file fits in with this existing workflow.
* Using CLI flags is a non-orthogonal solution. Only a limited number of options are available; using CLI options means that Pulp Smash makes use of both CLI and config file options; using CLI options pushes SSH options into Pulp Smash itself instead of leaving them nicely separated in an external configuration file; and CLI options override whatever is in a configuration file, meaning that we make Pulp Smash incompatible with certain environments.
* The complaint that we're forcing people to do a lot of configuration work seems incorrect to me. (Remember: copying and pasting three lines into a file.) Disagreeable? Yes. A burdensome waste of time? No.
* We haven't even brainstormed for other solutions yet. Hard-coded options are the second solution that has been proffered. The second! To jump on it now and vociferously decry pushback is short-sighted.

Does any of this matter? Yes, I think so. I imagine that repeated dismissals of unsupported platforms is one of the contributing factors that has made it so hard to install even on supported platforms. Let's not make that mistake again.

Finally: are there other solutions? I expect so. And if we discover them, we may find that we unexpectedly solve other, related problems. For example, I dislike how users are required to manually edit the Pulp Smash configuration file. (We're consuming users' real time.) A solution to that issue is to rework and expand `pulp_smash/__main__.py` and add a simple configuration wizard that creates a configuration file and/or `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/pulp_smash/ssh_config` and/or prints out lines the user may want to add to their `~/.ssh/config`. When Pulp Smash runs, it can look for and use that file if present. Whaddya know — a flexible solution that solves *two* time-eating problems, not just the one described at the head of this thread.

That all sounds good and well but you will have to open the issues and pull requests into the [django-ipware](https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware) project. I think that it is unreasonable to ask for the author to implement changes to only benefit `django-axes`, so contributing that new `django-ipware` utility would be the prompt thing to do, if you want it.

`django-axes` and `django-ipware` do not share the developers or code base, and `django-ipware` seems pretty well designed to me, in all honesty. Parsing end user IPs is a best effort task and can not be done with 100% reliability, and hence the end user IPs shouldn't be relied on too heavily in terms of authentication. They can be used to augment the authentication flow to distinguish forged authentication requests coming from a single computer and ban that IP, but they have little use outside of that.

The solution in `django-axes` at this time is to improve the documentation to include instructions on configuring `django-ipware` in a sane fashion. This can, in my opinion, be as simple as directing the user refer to the `django-ipware` in the [configuration documentation](https://django-axes.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration.html).

> Providing contrib submodule for drf wasn't core competency too

The contrib submodule pattern is pretty common across Django apps. In DOT's case, it's there to enhance DOT's compatibility with another very popular library which is likely to be used alongside it. It's very much within the realm of core competency.

What's *not* core competency is throttling and bruteforce prevention in general. These are complex problems, and in order to be production-ready they need to accomodate for varieties of setups. There's django libraries that do this better, there's DRF that does it well enough, there's http modules that do it well, there's Cloudflare, etc...

Otherwise, literally any django library that serves HTTP responses would be asked to implement its own throttling. You say nobly that you want to "solve the problem for others"; so solve it for every library out there, don't just blindly get stuck at the first one you encounter it with.

> maybe it's not a bad idea to improve the drf contrib submodule in DOT so that users can use DRF's native throttling with DOT too.

But that's my point, it's already possible... DRF lets you throttle whichever endpoints you like and is extremely flexible. So what exactly are you proposing?

@nibanks, some suggestions:

> Secondary goal:
>
>Design should provide ability to have the simplest device side code possible (PHY only for the most >part); not requiring any of the OpenThread library.

> The solution I am proposing is to allow for dynamically assigning callbacks. The only other solution (I could see) is to force everything to go through OpenThread, but that would go against the secondary goal and seemed to add a lot of unnecessary logic to OpenThread.

I think this can be realized with the following approach: We can have a separate makefile/build which excludes all the OpenThread code and includes a new file which defines the callbacks from radio platform (e.g. `otPlatRadioReceiveDone`) and hooks them to spinel code which then passes the raw frames up through the spinel/NCP code. With this model, there is no need to change the radio platform APIs (i.e., the callbacks remain the same and there is no need for dynamic change of callbacks. There are different implementations for the same callback/function in different builds). 

Another suggestion: We can consider defining a new radio abstraction APIs for this purpose (for the code that would run on the chip/radio and hooks to spinel to send raw frames to host). The current radio platform APIs were designed for OpenThread use-case and I think for the "direct radio to spinel" model a simpler radio abstraction may get the job done with even simpler/fewer APIs.

On the host (where the OpenThread stack runs) we need new code/module that parses/forms the spinel messages from/to radio chip and then present itself as an OpenThread platform radio (providing all the APIs in `radio.h`, ...). I think this can be added as another platform under `examples/platforms` or `examples/drivers` (just like posix, cc2538, etc.).

(Using @turon's `OT_TOOL_*` prefix in this post after an in-person discussion on the matter)

Given that the IAR seems to be the only compiler that enforces the use of the packed flag as a prefix (meaning that it's use as a prefix is more bug than feature), I am very sympathetic to @turon's asymmetrical "`OT_TOOL_PACKED_BEFORE`/`OT_TOOL_PACKED`" notation, since it allows for `OT_TOOL_PACKED_BEFORE` to possibly be retired, leaving `OT_TOOL_PACKED` to remain unchanged.

However, I think there is a credible argument that adding `OT_TOOL_PACKED_AFTER` encourages the programmer to realize that there is likely something that needs to come `OT_TOOL_PACKED_BEFORE`. It would be very easy for a programmer to simply ignore the `OT_TOOL_PACKED_BEFORE` macro and think that they are doing just fine with `OT_TOOL_PACKED`.

So after thinking about this more, I think having a `OT_TOOL_PACKED_BEFORE` and a `OT_TOOL_PACKED_AFTER` (and no `OT_TOOL_PACKED`) makes sense from the perspective of avoiding potential (and very hard to diagnose) bugs.

@dhait, I maintain a `cmd2` based application which does exactly what you describe. The relevant code is shown in https://github.com/tomcatmanager/tomcatmanager/blob/master/tomcatmanager/__main__.py.

There is a big more going on in there than you have asked for, including using `argparse` to process command line arguments from the shell invocation. It also checks the results of the `cmd2` command and sets a shell exit code, which you may or may not want to do.

This code is in ``__main.py__`` because `tomcatmanager` uses a feature of `setuptools` to install command line executives as described at http://python-packaging.readthedocs.io/en/latest/command-line-scripts.html

The resulting behavior is just what you requested:
```
$ tomcatmanager help
```
executes the `help` command and exits, while
```
$ tomcatmanager
```
enters the interactive command loop.

```
cmd2
| cmd2
| | __init__.py
| | cmd2.py
| tests
| |  __init__.py
| | test_cmd2.py
| | test_completion.py
| setup.py
| pytest.ini
| tox.ini
```

This is great, thank you for the quick fix and the detailed discussion of the issue! The concept described in the caster overhaul sounds sensible and like a good solution on the longer run. How would `keep_alive`s be handled in that case transparently? There are multiple scenarios possible:

* No `keep_alive`: dependencies don't have to be stored, no problem.
* `keep_alive` fully applied: the unconverted and the converted objects are being kept alive. Advantage: transparent for the library user, because the object that is provided as argument 'really' stays alive in any case, not only the converted one. Downside: maybe it's not necessary to keep both alive and it would be sufficient to keep the converted one, memory overhead.
* `keep_alive` only applies to the possibly converted object. Advantage: memory efficient. Disadvantage: maybe the user expects the object to stick around, due to gc effects, destructor operations, etc. Broadly speaking, this may not be what the user 'semantically' expects when using `keep_alive`.

Maybe two versions of `keep_alive` would be appropriate, transporting this choice to the user. Or am I thinking along the wrong lines here?

Right, as you say, you need to monkeypatch, or use async aware apis/tools (e.g. like https://github.com/saghul/erequests or https://github.com/kennethreitz/grequests).

Gunicorn actually enables monkeypatching for you, see:

https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/blob/master/gunicorn/workers/geventlet.py#L90
https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/blob/master/gunicorn/workers/ggevent.py#L57

Interestingly, your test app works fine for me in py 3.5, but not in py 3.6, which is very odd. But looking around, I see this issue has been encountered:

https://github.com/requests/requests/issues/3752
https://github.com/gevent/gevent/issues/903
https://github.com/eventlet/eventlet/issues/371 (you seem to have commented on this one! :)

So, my reading of this is that, on python 3.6, requests has to be imported *after* monkey patching, to workaround this bug. At least until some later version of requests. I tested with 2.17.3, and the bug is still present.

So, this is a bit tricky to solve, as talisker imports requests early. I think we may need to rework talisker's requests integration to lazily import requests, rather than when talisker.requests is imported. I will take a stab it ASAP.

I agree that Lexicon should be an implementation detail. I only went for `--dns-lexicon` &c. to present an easy way forward; what I would like ideally is more like this: ```sh certbot certonly \ --dns ~/.secrets/certbot/vultr.ini \ --dns-propagation-seconds 10 \ -d example.com \ -d www.example.com ``` … with the same vultr.ini; and let the DNS authenticator plugin manager look at the header line in the INI file, see “vultr”, and look to see if there is a certbot-dns-vultr package installed (however you like), and if there is not, pass it on to Lexicon, and if *it* fails too, then complain to the user. I think that mitigates some of your problems with my initial suggestion. The main hazard I can think of is if for some reason you have a custom plugin, *and* Lexicon can handles that provider, and the custom plugin isn’t installed for whatever reason, so the Lexicon one is used instead, unbeknownst to the user. But I tend to think Certbot should be aiming for *total* delegation to Lexicon, so that the certbot-dns-* package check and use could then be dropped altogether, leaving no scope for surprises. That would entail moving some things out of this repository into it (e.g. certbot-dns-rfc2136). I only skimmed them very briefly, but my passing glance showed only two variations in the Lexicon-backed DNS authentication plugins: default propagation delay (mostly I imagine they’re just a figment of copying and pasting, but they could potentially have been adjusted for slow providers), and authentication error handing. Both of these seem to me like they should live in Lexicon—as a recommended propagation delay (just another constant on each provider), and as a new type of exception if there isn’t one for the purpose already. It seems to me that Certbot *shouldn’t* be adding anything extra to Lexicon—its DNS authentication truly should be a very thin layer on top of Lexicon in general. Doing it all like this will require a bit of work on Lexicon as well as a substantial adjustment of how it works in certbot, but as an outsider to both projects this seems the sensible approach to me. We could work inside the current architecture to reduce the eight hundred lines I had to change to just two or three with code generation, but it seems to me much more useful to generalise it so that there *is* only one plugin, for almost all cases at least. I recall hearing rumours of Lexicon’s route53 provider being broken, while I’m guessing Certbot’s isn’t—that would certainly be cause for keeping certbot-dns-route53 separate in the short term! At this point, my impression is that testing is the main concern (absolutely genuine; it’s mine too), and a concern about upstream breakages and security issues (misguided, in my opinion: the new workflow would be for Certbot to specify a dependency on a particular version of Lexicon, ideally bundling it when installed via certbot-auto, and so you still have an opportune time for such review, when updating it—for all plugins at once, rather than a harum-scarum approach that will have you review inconsistently; alternatively, stop viewing it as “upstream” and review more proactively).

Following @fserra, you need an auxiliary variable `c` which represents the minimum between the variable `a` and `b` in your model `m`:
```m.addCons(c == ((a+b)/2 - abs(a-b)/2)```
Same holds for the maximum, changing the sign before `abs`:
```m.addCons(c == ((a+b)/2 + abs(a-b)/2)```

I could consider to implement `min` and `max` this way if they are native SCIP features too. Is that OK for the maintainers?

https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/new_format/src/sos/tasks.py#L137

There is a fixed size header for task files. sos can read/write important information to the header without loading the entire file. E.g. get status by reading [a few bytes from the file](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/new_format/src/sos/tasks.py#L348) although I am checking the 10 byte string to an integer soon.

The header also have "size" of different blocks so that task parameters and `stdout`, `stderr` can be retrieved separately. `stdout`, `stderr` are compressed to save diskspace.

With this change, task status are written directly to the task file and there is less a need to "guess" the status of the task under different situations, and information such as time for status change is also recorded so that it is possible to learn the history of the task.

I think the issue here is that the local scheduler attached to the killed plasma manager never detects that it's lost the connection. Normally, the local scheduler contacts the plasma manager at an interval to fetch any missing object dependencies. In this case, it doesn't contact the plasma manager because all task dependencies are fulfilled after the first task, and so the local scheduler never learns that its associated plasma manager is dead. Then, the task never gets reconstructed since that local scheduler still holds a lock on the task in the task table.

We should probably fix this by having the local scheduler detect whether a dead plasma manager is associated with itself during reconstruction.

My guess is that there's an inherent deadlock in your program, and going faster has exposed it. Are you using queues? Are you using control dependencies? Those are two common ways of ending up with deadlocks.

You can patch over the problem by adding timeout in session.run, and retrying. Here's a way (along with some options to disable various optimizations)

```
def create_session():
  optimizer_options = tf.OptimizerOptions(opt_level=tf.OptimizerOptions.L0)
  config = tf.ConfigProto(operation_timeout_in_ms=15000, graph_options=tf.GraphOptions(optimizer_options=optimizer_options))
  config.graph_options.rewrite_options.constant_folding = rewriter_config_pb2.RewriterConfig.OFF
  config.graph_options.place_pruned_graph = True
  return tf.Session(config=config)
```

There must be an easy way for plugins to define options. The command line should be able to detect these options and list them in the help dialog. `streamlink help twitch` for instance.

So for instance the twitch plugin could have `oauth_token`, `cookie`, and `disable-hosting` options. In the API, these are set using the `plugin.set_option(name, val)`.`

I see two ways for the CLI to handle plugin specific options. The first one is to use automatic namespacing. Using the twitch example again, its option could be specified using the `--twitch-` namespace, which is generated automatically using the plugin's name. Example  `--twitch-disable-hosting`. The options are not namespaced in the API, since you know which plugin you are working with.

The second option would be to not have namespacing. Since streamlink always uses a single plugin, there's no possibility of a plugin specific option overlapping with one from another plugin. The main issue I see with this approach is that the semantics could get messy if, say, `--disable-hosting` has a different meaning based on the plugin.

> I find its naming very natural - it indeed waits for result within specified period of time

My complaint about its name is that in `await foo()` versus `await wait_for(foo(), 60)`, the `await` is what does the waiting, and the complete and only purpose for `wait_for` is to add a timeout. So shouldn't the name say something about timeouts? It's not at all obvious if you're skimming the docs trying to figure out how to add a timeout :-).

> `move_on_after()`

The problem with exceptions is that they don't compose well. Suppose you have multiple nested cancel scopes. An exception could tell you that some timeout somewhere expired, but it doesn't tell you whether it's the particular timeout you wanted to check for.

I played around with some options that use exceptions, but it just makes things even messier. Like compare:

```python
with move_on_after(60) as cancel_scope:
    await foo()
if cancel_scope.cancelled_caught:
    await recover_from_foo_timeout()
```

to some mess like

```python
try:
    with move_on_after(60) as cancel_scope:
        await foo()
except TimeoutError as exc:
    if exc.cancel_scope is cancel_scope:
        await recover_from_foo_timeout()
    else:
        raise
```

Also, `move_on_after` semantics turn out to be useful surprisingly often. Basically it's the cases where you're like "whatever, I don't care about this anymore, so forget about it" – it's not an error, you just want to move on. And the low-level cancellation semantics work this way, just because when you want to have two options, one that does something and one that doesn't, it's usually easier to build the do-something option on top of the do-nothing option, rather than vice-versa.

But yeah, if you want the "set a timeout and throw an error if it expires" then `fail_after` does that for you.

So first, as an FYI, your example code can be simplified a lot by using Trio's high-level networking interface – it's kinda new and not documented as well as it could be yet, so I'm not surprised you missed it, but this is definitely the recommended way to go:

```python
async def echo_server(server_stream):
    try:
        # Normally, this 'with' isn't really necessary, because serve_tcp will automatically
        # close server_stream when the echo_server function returns. But it is needed to
        # trigger the problem that this issue is about :-).
        async with server_stream:
            await server_stream.receive_some(BUFSIZE)
            raise RuntimeError('Damn!')
    except Exception as exc:
        await trio.sleep(0.1) # make problem deterministic
        log.exception('Things went wrong...')

@pytest.mark.trio
async def test_server(nursery):
    await nursery.start(partial(trio.serve_tcp, PORT, echo_server, host="127.0.0.1"))
    # nursery.start and trio.serve_tcp coordinate to make sure that we don't continue
    # until the server is ready to accept connections, so no need to sleep here
    async with trio.open_tcp_stream("127.0.0.1", PORT) as client_stream:
        await client_stream.send_all(b'hello, world')
        resp = await client_stream.receive_some(BUFSIZE)
        assert resp == b'hello, world'
```

This also lets makes it easy to handle IPv6 and SSL by just changing the serve_tcp call. You could also let the OS automatically pick a free port by making it:

```python
listeners = await nursery.start(partial(trio.serve_tcp, 0, echo_server, host="127.0.0.1"))
async with trio.testing.open_stream_to_socket_listener(listeners[0]) as client_stream:
    ...
```

I think I'm also required by the ancient laws governing socket programmers to point out that this test is broken in any case, because you can't assume that the call to `receive_some` will get all of the data back – even if the echo server was actually working, it could recieve `b"he"` first, and then `b"llo world"` later, or something like that. I know it's not the point here, but it's good to get in the habit of handling these things correctly, because it's the sort of thing that will seem to work in simple tests over localhost but then fail randomly in real life.

Anyway, none of that has to do with your actual problem, I just wanted to point that out :-).

So regarding your actual issue, what's happening here is:

1. The client sends `b"hello world"`
1. The server receives `b"hello world"`, but then instead of echoing it back, it raises an exception.
1. The exception causes us to exit the `with server_sock` block, which closes the server's socket.
1. The server's `except` block then catches the exception, and blocks in the call to `trio.sleep`.
1. The server's socket being closed causes the client's call to `recv` to return an empty string `b""`, which just means that the server has hung up.
1. The client compares this to the string it was expecting, `b"hello world"`, and since they don't match it raises an exception.
1. The test machinery gets this exception and cancels everything in the nursery fixture, including in particular our `echo_server` task.
1. The server's `trio.sleep` call raises `Cancelled`, so the `log.exception` line is never executed. The `Cancelled` exception does at least have the `RuntimeError` as its `__context__`...
1. ...but then the `Cancelled` exception is absorbed by nursery fixture, instead of being printed, so the `__context__` is lost.

This is tricky!

The key features of the server that make this happen seem to be: it raises an error, the error handling ends up sending a message to the client (in this case by closing the socket), and then after that the error handling tries to report something, but in between those it has a checkpoint where it can be cancelled.

So in your original example, the call to `trio.sleep` in the server actually is crucial, because without that there would be no checkpoint – the socket would be closed and then we'd immediately log the error, without giving the client a chance to run or checking for cancellation. But this isn't a general solution; in my revised version, you *can* get the issue without the sleep, because the `async with` introduces a checkpoint at the critical place.

Though you could fix that case too, by moving the `async with` outside of the `try`/`except`. But that's not a general solution either; assuming the server is running some arbitrary code, it may well have some kind of cleanup handlers inside that execute checkpoints, and that's all we need here for the original exception to get lost.

Really this problem is just inherent in how exceptions work. If you block inside an `except` block, then that needs to be cancellable, and when it's cancelled you'll probably lose the old exception, which is unfortunate if you were planning to re-raise it (like the `__aexit__` handler would in `async with stream`), or do something else like log it (as in the original example). But we can't just not cancel a task that's blocked, and we can't tell what the task would have done with the original exception if we hadn't cancelled it. If we were in a language like Rust where errors propagation happens explicitly then this wouldn't be as much of an issue. But we can't require cancellations be propagated explicitly; without assistance from the language no-one would do it and we'd just have tons of programs that couldn't shut down.

Maybe what we really need here is some kind of better tracking of exception `__context__`? I'm going to open an issue in trio to consider that further.

I agree that the only plausible workaround at the pytest-trio level is do make the test running code look something like:

```python
try:
    await actual_test_fn(**kwargs)
except Exception:
    await trio.sleep(0.1)
    raise
```

I am... really unsure about whether this is a good idea. Running tests fast is **really** important, and adding a sleep to every failure will slow down everyone, even though I suspect that this particular case is quite rare. Though that's another thing that makes me nervous: we have no idea how often this happens or any way to figure out whether this hack is actually helping. Is 0.1 seconds even long enough? Is it way too long? How do we know?

1) develop (all odd)

The benefit is obvious, we never need to promise any long term support for develop opset version.

Hi @iskandr ,
Yes. This would be a tricky PR (or series of PRs), if indeed you would want go on this route.
Because I am a regular user of `pyensembl` and I like how it works, in favour of the PRs, I would argue that   
(1) Firstly, the modifications only pertain to the installation process so main functionalities of `pyensembl` would remain the same. I have tested this.  
(2) The modifications such as the usage of a dataframe (`Species.dspecies`) as a portable registry of genomes, opens new ways to import/export and share collection of genomes across users.  
and finally
(3) I think explicitly mentioning (1) species name (2) reference name (assembly) and (3) Ensembl release would make installation of genomes more convenient for user (if at all it's a big effort in the first place). Because then they don't have to look for which assembly and release was automatically downloaded by `pyensembl`. 

Therefore, I would be happy to help in PRs. 

Regarding,
> without human genome info popping up now than then.

sorry, I really did not make it clear there. I wanted to say that if defaults of inputs are set to `human` (eg. [1](https://github.com/openvax/pyensembl/blob/718b2f98f21ea0fc0b19ac13a9b653b8d3b5eb30/pyensembl/ensembl_release.py#L54), [2](https://github.com/openvax/pyensembl/blob/718b2f98f21ea0fc0b19ac13a9b653b8d3b5eb30/pyensembl/ensembl_release.py#L73) and [3](https://github.com/openvax/pyensembl/blob/718b2f98f21ea0fc0b19ac13a9b653b8d3b5eb30/pyensembl/ensembl_url_templates.py#L41)) and if somebody working with other species uses the those pieces of codes, without specifying the defaults, they may find `human` related information popping up unexpectedly. I hope I am making it clear. It may not be that important because I suppose this potential issue would have been considered before assigning defaults to `human` at those places. However, in my opinion, removing them would make things stricter and absolute.

Best,
Rohan.

In pip 10, all API has moved to `pip._internal` , and that [`pip.__init__.py`](https://github.com/pypa/pip/blob/master/src/pip/__init__.py) only contains version info.
So use the following code to import origin `main` command.

```python

try:
    pip._internal import main
except ImportError:
    from pip import main
```

As the private module `_internal`, these code may not guaranteed and any change should be concerned.
